content.text: Why Chunk Size Is the Most Impactful RAG Parameter

Chunk size determines what the embedding model sees, what the retriever returns, and how much context the generator receives. Every downstream quality metric — retrieval precision, faithfulness, answer completeness — is affected by this single parameter. Too small and the embedding is specific but lacks the surrounding context needed to generate a complete answer. Too large and the embedding becomes diluted, precision drops, and the context window fills with irrelevant content. The optimal chunk size is not universal — it depends on your document structure, query patterns, and embedding model.

attrs.title: Small vs large chunks — trade-offs and typical sweet spot

content.text: Splitting Strategies and Overlap

LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter is the standard choice for most corpora. It tries to split on paragraph breaks first, then sentence breaks, then word breaks — preserving semantic units where possible. The chunk_overlap parameter ensures that a sentence split across two chunks appears in both, preventing the retriever from missing a phrase that straddles a boundary.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# Baseline configuration — tune chunk_size and chunk_overlap empirically
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,       # Characters, not tokens (approx 128 tokens for English)
    chunk_overlap=50,     # Overlap prevents missing content at boundaries
    length_function=len,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " ", ""],  # Priority order
)

with open("./docs/policy.txt") as f:
    text = f.read()

chunks = splitter.create_documents([text])
print(f"Split into {len(chunks)} chunks")
print(f"Avg chunk length: {sum(len(c.page_content) for c in chunks) / len(chunks):.0f} chars")

# Token-aware splitting (more precise than character count)
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # Used by text-embedding-3-* and gpt-4o

token_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder(
    model_name="text-embedding-3-small",
    chunk_size=256,    # Tokens
    chunk_overlap=30,  # Tokens
)
token_chunks = token_splitter.create_documents([text])
print(f"Token-split: {len(token_chunks)} chunks, "
      f"avg {sum(len(enc.encode(c.page_content)) for c in token_chunks)//len(token_chunks)} tokens")
Document-Structure-Aware Splitting

For Markdown, HTML, or code, splitting on structural boundaries (headings, sections, functions) produces more coherent chunks than splitting on character count alone. LangChain provides structure-specific splitters for these formats.

from langchain_text_splitters import (
    MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter,
    HTMLHeaderTextSplitter,
    Language,
    RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter,
)

# Markdown: split on headers first, then fall back to size
markdown_splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(
    headers_to_split_on=[
        ("#",  "h1"),
        ("##", "h2"),
        ("###","h3"),
    ],
    strip_headers=False,  # Keep headers in chunk for context
)

md_text = """
# Return Policy
## Electronics
Electronics can be returned within 30 days.
## Clothing
Clothing can be returned within 60 days.
"""

header_chunks = markdown_splitter.split_text(md_text)
for chunk in header_chunks:
    print(chunk.metadata, "→", chunk.page_content[:60])
# {'h1': 'Return Policy', 'h2': 'Electronics'} → Electronics can be returned within 30 days.
# {'h1': 'Return Policy', 'h2': 'Clothing'} → Clothing can be returned within 60 days.

# Code: split on function/class boundaries
code_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language(
    language=Language.PYTHON,
    chunk_size=400,
    chunk_overlap=0,  # Code functions shouldn't overlap
)
Empirically Tuning Chunk Size

The only reliable way to find the optimal chunk size for your corpus is to run a grid search across candidate sizes and measure retrieval precision and faithfulness on a representative evaluation set. Build a pipeline that re-chunks, re-embeds, re-indexes, and re-evaluates at each candidate size, then pick the size that maximizes the metric most important for your application.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_core.documents import Document


def build_index(docs: list[Document], chunk_size: int, chunk_overlap: int) -> FAISS:
    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=chunk_size, chunk_overlap=chunk_overlap
    )
    chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
    embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
    return FAISS.from_documents(chunks, embeddings)


def evaluate_chunk_size(
    raw_docs: list[Document],
    eval_set: list[dict],   # [{"question": str, "relevant_ids": set[str]}]
    chunk_sizes: list[int],
    chunk_overlap: int = 50,
    k: int = 5,
) -> dict[int, dict[str, float]]:
    results = {}
    for size in chunk_sizes:
        index = build_index(raw_docs, size, chunk_overlap)
        retriever = index.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": k})

        precisions, recalls = [], []
        for item in eval_set:
            retrieved = retriever.invoke(item["question"])
            ret_ids = {d.metadata.get("id", d.page_content[:50]) for d in retrieved}
            rel_ids = item["relevant_ids"]
            hits = len(ret_ids & rel_ids)
            precisions.append(hits / k)
            recalls.append(hits / len(rel_ids) if rel_ids else 0.0)

        results[size] = {
            "precision_at_k": round(sum(precisions) / len(precisions), 3),
            "recall_at_k":    round(sum(recalls) / len(recalls), 3),
        }
        print(f"chunk_size={size:4d}: P@{k}={results[size]['precision_at_k']:.3f}, "
              f"R@{k}={results[size]['recall_at_k']:.3f}")

    best_size = max(results, key=lambda s: results[s]["precision_at_k"])
    print(f"\nBest chunk size: {best_size} tokens")
    return results


# Run grid search
evaluate_chunk_size(raw_docs, eval_set, chunk_sizes=[128, 256, 512, 768, 1024])
Parent-Child Chunking: Best of Both Sizes

Parent-child chunking addresses the precision-vs-context trade-off directly: embed small child chunks for high-precision retrieval, but return the larger parent chunk to the generator for richer context. The child chunk's specificity ensures it matches the query; the parent chunk provides the surrounding context needed for a complete answer.

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Child splitter: small chunks for precise embedding
child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=200, chunk_overlap=20)

# Parent splitter: larger chunks returned to the generator
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800, chunk_overlap=80)

# Vector store holds child chunk embeddings; doc store holds parent chunks
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings, collection_name="child_chunks")
docstore = InMemoryStore()  # Replace with Redis for production

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)

# Add documents — parent chunks are stored, children are embedded
retriever.add_documents(raw_docs)

# Retrieve — search is over small children, but returns large parents
results = retriever.invoke("What is the return window for electronics?")
print(f"Returned {len(results)} parent chunks")
print(f"First result length: {len(results[0].page_content)} chars")  # ~800 chars
Metadata-Enriched Chunks

Every chunk should carry metadata that enables both filtering and source attribution. At minimum, include the source document path, the section heading it came from, and its position in the document. This metadata lets you filter retrievals to specific document types and cite the exact source in the generated answer.

from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_text_splitters import MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter, RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
import hashlib


def split_with_metadata(
    text: str,
    source_path: str,
    chunk_size: int = 512,
    chunk_overlap: int = 50,
) -> list[Document]:
    """Split a document and enrich each chunk with metadata."""
    # Step 1: Split on Markdown headers to capture section context
    md_splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(
        headers_to_split_on=[("#", "h1"), ("##", "h2"), ("###", "h3")],
        strip_headers=False,
    )
    header_chunks = md_splitter.split_text(text)

    # Step 2: Further split large header sections on size
    size_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=chunk_size, chunk_overlap=chunk_overlap
    )

    final_chunks: list[Document] = []
    for i, hchunk in enumerate(header_chunks):
        sub_chunks = size_splitter.create_documents(
            [hchunk.page_content],
            metadatas=[hchunk.metadata],
        )
        for j, sub in enumerate(sub_chunks):
            # Stable chunk ID for deduplication and caching
            chunk_id = hashlib.md5(
                f"{source_path}:{i}:{j}:{sub.page_content[:100]}".encode()
            ).hexdigest()[:12]
            sub.metadata.update({
                "source":    source_path,
                "chunk_id":  chunk_id,
                "chunk_idx": len(final_chunks),
            })
            final_chunks.append(sub)

    return final_chunks


chunks = split_with_metadata(md_text, source_path="docs/return_policy.md")
for c in chunks[:2]:
    print(c.metadata)
    print(c.page_content[:80])
    print()
Overlap Strategy: When More Overlap Helps

Overlap prevents information loss at chunk boundaries but increases index size and redundancy. The right overlap depends on the sentence length in your corpus. A good heuristic: set overlap to the average sentence length in your documents — typically 20–60 tokens for English prose. For code, zero overlap is often better because function boundaries are natural split points that should not bleed across chunks.

import statistics
import re
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")


def recommend_overlap(text: str, fraction: float = 0.15) -> int:
    """
    Recommend chunk_overlap as a fraction of average sentence length.
    fraction=0.15 means overlap covers ~15% of the average sentence.
    """
    sentences = re.split(r"(?<=[.!?])\s+", text)
    token_lengths = [len(enc.encode(s)) for s in sentences if len(s.split()) > 3]
    if not token_lengths:
        return 30  # Safe default
    avg_sentence_tokens = statistics.mean(token_lengths)
    recommended = max(10, int(avg_sentence_tokens * fraction))
    return min(recommended, 100)  # Cap at 100 tokens


# Example: technical documentation with long sentences → higher overlap
tech_text = open("docs/policy.txt").read() if False else "Policy text here. Another sentence."
overlap = recommend_overlap(tech_text)
print(f"Recommended overlap: {overlap} tokens")

# Build splitter with corpus-tuned overlap
tuned_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder(
    model_name="text-embedding-3-small",
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=overlap,
)
Chunk Size by Document Type
Different document types have different information densities and natural unit sizes. Using a single chunk size for all document types leaves performance on the table.
Document Type
Recommended Size
Overlap
Rationale
FAQ / Q&A
128–256 tokens
0–20 tokens
Each Q&A pair is a natural unit; no cross-boundary context needed
Technical docs / manuals
256–512 tokens
30–50 tokens
Sections are cohesive but benefit from surrounding context
Research papers / reports
512–1024 tokens
50–100 tokens
Dense prose; arguments span paragraphs
Source code
Per-function / per-class
0 tokens
Functions are natural units; overlap creates duplicate code fragments
Summary
Chunk size is the single most impactful RAG parameter — it affects embedding specificity, retrieval precision, and answer completeness simultaneously.
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with 256–512 tokens and 30–50 token overlap is a solid default; token-aware splitting is more precise than character count.
Use structure-aware splitters (MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter, Language splitter) when your documents have natural structural boundaries.
Run a grid search over candidate sizes measuring Precision@K and Recall@K on your actual eval set — the optimal size varies by corpus.
ParentDocumentRetriever embeds small chunks for precision but returns large parent chunks to the generator — the best of both worlds.
